title: Racism & Prejudice
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Racism and Prejudice
summary: Scripture declares that all people are made in God's image, dismantles ethnic hierarchies through Christ, and calls believers to actively love across every human-constructed boundary.
body:
The Bible's opening chapter establishes the foundation: every human being is made in the image of God [verse:gen.1.27]. There is no hierarchy of image-bearing — no race, ethnicity, or people group carries more of the divine likeness than another. Any system that assigns different value to people based on their skin color or origin contradicts the Creator's design.

The early church was forced to confront this directly. Peter's vision of the unclean animals taught him what he had been slow to learn: God does not show favoritism, and no person should be called impure or unclean [verse:acts.10.34-35]. The barrier between Jew and Gentile — the deepest ethnic divide of the ancient world — was demolished by Christ, who made the two groups into one new humanity [verse:eph.2.14-16].

Paul articulates the principle at its most expansive: in Christ there is neither Jew nor Gentile, slave nor free, male nor female — all are one [verse:gal.3.28]. This does not erase difference but removes it as a basis for superiority or exclusion.

James addresses the practical outworking. If you show favoritism, you sin [verse:jas.2.8-9]. The royal law — love your neighbor as yourself — cannot coexist with preferential treatment based on appearance, status, or origin. The prophets demanded the same: Amos thundered against nations that treated people as commodities, and Micah summarized God's requirement as justice, mercy, and humble walking with God [verse:mic.6.8].

verses.annotation: Every person bears God's image — the theological foundation for the equal dignity of all people.
In Christ, the deepest human divisions — ethnic, social, gender — lose their power to separate or rank.
Peter's revelation that God shows no favoritism and accepts people from every nation.
Christ breaks down the dividing wall between ethnic groups, creating one new humanity.
Favoritism is sin — the royal law of love prohibits preferential treatment.
God's requirement summarized: do justice, love mercy, walk humbly.
The heavenly vision of every nation, tribe, and tongue worshiping together — diversity as God's design, not a problem to solve.

scholars.quote: Keener argues that the early church's struggle to integrate Gentiles was the defining social crisis of the first century, and that Acts 10 represents the theological breakthrough that made the universal church possible.

Lincoln reads Ephesians 2:14-16 as Paul's most radical social statement — the cross does not merely forgive individuals but creates an entirely new community where former enemies share a common identity.

Davids emphasizes that James 2 treats favoritism as a violation of the whole law, not a minor social misstep — partiality based on appearance fundamentally contradicts the character of the God who chose the poor.
